summary: Gruber reviews the iPad Air, arguing that its dramatic weight reduction fundamentally changes how users should think about iPad sizing decisions. He frames iPad usage around two groups: those who carry an iPad as a supplement to a laptop, and those who can use an iPad as their primary portable computer. For the first group, the Air's weight now makes the full-size iPad competitive with the Mini as a travel companion. For the second group, he presents benchmark data showing the iPad Air outperforms a 2010 MacBook Air, arguing it's a legitimate laptop replacement for many users. He concludes that the iPad is not a separate product category but is actively taking over a large segment of the PC industry.
key_points: The iPad Air's 1.0-pound weight crosses a psychological threshold — less than half the weight of a MacBook Air — making it viable both as a laptop supplement and replacement
Benchmark comparisons show the iPad Air outperforms a late-2010 MacBook Air in both Geekbench and Sunspider, demonstrating that a brand-new iPad now beats a three-year-old laptop
The weight reduction significantly diminishes the iPad Mini's primary advantage, making the full-size iPad competitive for users who previously chose the Mini for portability
Gruber argues the iPad is not a separate 'tablet' category but is actively cannibalizing the PC market, and the iPad Air will accelerate this trend
The Smart Case is criticized as pointless — why buy an amazingly thin device only to make it thicker with a case you never remove?
money_quotes: The iPad Air makes an iPad 3/4 feel heavy; it makes an 11-inch MacBook Air feel like an anchor.
Good god almighty did I miss having a retina display on my daily iPad.

An obsession with treating 'tablets' as an entirely new and separate product category is blinding some observers from what is really going on with the iPad — it is taking over a large segment of the PC industry.

For anyone who doesn't truly take advantage of the capabilities in Mac OS X (or Windows) that aren't available in iOS, the iPad Air is a superior portable computer to a laptop in nearly every way. Smaller, lighter, simpler, more fun.
What's the point of buying an iPad that is so amazingly thin only to wrap it in a case that makes it so much thicker?
In short, for people upgrading from a 3 or 4 year-old laptop, the iPad Air is faster, straight up.
The new iPad Air is a full-fledged competitor to laptops.
Starting with the original model in 2010, Apple has seemingly been unwilling to bend on a floor of 10 hours of battery life, and the iPad Air maintains this pedigree.
key_insight: The iPad Air's weight reduction and performance gains collapse the distinction between tablet and laptop, positioning it not as a new product category but as the next evolution of the personal computer for most users.
